claimed_contribution: The paper refines prior multilayer feedforward neural network methods by explicitly addressing the bias-variance dilemma and using v-fold cross-validation to improve generalizability.
It evaluates whether neural-network-based decision support can outperform traditional prediction methods for NPD project outcomes under multiple decision criteria.
It claims to be the first study to apply artificial neural network data analysis to the NPD process and to create, train, and validate MFN-based decision support systems for project selection.
context: The study is set in firm new product development project screening and resource allocation, especially go/no-go decisions under scarce resources. The empirical setting uses U.S. high-technology firms, with an additional illustration from a major manufacturing firm.
design_and_data: The main data set includes 612 NPD projects introduced by U.S. high-technology firms after 1991; project outcomes were classified by contact people and other executives using firm minimum profitability criteria. The projects were randomly split into a training sample (n = 400) and a holdout evaluation sample (n = 212), with pairing of successful and unsuccessful projects from the same firm when possible. Each network used 43 input variables and predicted one output at a time (dichotomous success, financial success, or technical success); a two-stage MFN approach used 10-fold cross-validation to choose structure (43-5-1) and Levenberg-Marquardt training, with final predictions averaged across 10 trained networks. Holdout performance was compared with k-nearest neighbor, multinomial logit, OLS regression, and discriminant analysis as applicable, and an independent 23-project firm case was used for illustration.
main_findings: On the 212-project holdout sample, the MFN model produced the lowest SSE and MSE among compared methods for dichotomous, financial, and technical success predictions.
For dichotomous go/no-go prediction, the MFN correctly classified 96.7% of projects, exceeding k-nearest neighbor (92.5%), multinomial logit (85.4%), OLS (79.2%), and discriminant analysis (79.2%).

For three-category outcomes, the MFN yielded far fewer misclassifications than OLS (average 12.5% vs 60.4%), and in the 23-project case study it achieved 87.0%, 95.7%, and 73.9% correct predictions for dichotomous, financial, and technical outcomes, respectively.
one_line_summary: Using 612 U.S. high-technology NPD projects, the authors build and test an MFN neural-network decision support system that outperforms several traditional methods in predicting project outcomes and illustrate its managerial use in an independent firm case.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior marketing neural network comparison work by refining MFN methodology for generalizability and applying it to NPD project selection, which the authors describe as a new ANN application in this literature.
research_gap: Although neural networks had shown promising forecasting performance, prior work called for more comparative evidence on their predictive performance versus traditional methods in marketing settings. The authors also identify a lack of ANN-based, trained and validated decision support systems for NPD project selection.
research_question: The paper asks whether artificial neural network decision support systems can outperform traditional methods when predicting NPD project outcomes. It tests this at three levels: statistical error criteria (SSE and MSE), dichotomous go/no-go outcomes, and three-category outcomes (success, inconclusive, failure).
